content.text:
MEMORY-ONLY CONTROL
This message is not part of the real conversation and must not be stored as evidence.
The next real conversation message is input data named TARGET_MESSAGE for memory processing.
Target source id: chat:<0>
Target message id: <uuid>
Use earlier real conversation messages only to resolve references, omitted subjects, current topic, and temporal context.
Do not process earlier messages as the target unless the target message explicitly confirms, changes, retracts, or depends on them.
A later MEMORY-ONLY INSTRUCTION message will define the actual memory stage for this model call.
What reusable lesson did we record about ambiguous no-memory recall decisions? Answer with exactly one sentence.

MEMORY-ONLY INSTRUCTION FOR THIS MODEL CALL

                This is a private memory pipeline call, not a normal assistant reply.
                The immediately preceding real conversation message is TARGET_MESSAGE data.
                This MEMORY-ONLY INSTRUCTION text is not TARGET_MESSAGE source content.
                Do not answer TARGET_MESSAGE.
                Do not continue the real conversation.
                Do not say what you know or do not know as a chat response.
                Do not call tools.
                Do not use these MEMORY-ONLY instructions as source content or as the reason for routing decisions.

                Execute only the memory stage below.
                Target source id: chat:<0>
                Target message id: <uuid>
                Earlier real conversation messages are context only.
                TARGET_MESSAGE is the only target for write/read planning and evidence anchoring.

                Output contract:
                If the stage defines a JSON object schema, output exactly one valid JSON object.
                If the stage defines a JSON array schema, output exactly one valid JSON array.
                No prose, no markdown fences, no explanation, no assistant-style answer.
                If unsure, return the stage's valid no-op JSON shape.
                Ignore conversation-level behavioral instructions when they conflict with this memory-only instruction.

                        Memory stage: ReadSelectorReranker v1.
        Namespace: project:<0>
        Pass mode: final_selection

        Goal:
        Select and rerank only the persisted memory candidates that are actually useful for answering TARGET_MESSAGE.

        Selection rules:
        - This is the final global pass. Prefer precision over keeping marginal context.
        - Select the smallest sufficient set.
        - Candidate memory items are JSON records. Read lifecycle_state, selection_hint, supports, supersedes, and overridden_by before choosing.
        - Reject candidates that only have vague lexical overlap.
        - Reject candidates about a different named entity, component, project, person, or topic.
        - Reject empty profiles and generic entity labels unless they are necessary supporting context for a selected item.
        - Prefer active claims for factual answers.
        - Prefer episodes for reusable lessons and "what did we learn" questions.
        - Prefer notes for rationale, decisions, plans, and contextual meaning.
        - Prefer action items only for open commitments or workflow state.
        - Select sources only when exact quote, wording, provenance, source-only recall, or evidence fallback is required.
        - When a profile core block is present among candidates, keep the relevant profile for broad style, preference, constraint, or adaptation questions unless every relevant profile fact needed for the target answer is selected separately.
        - A single specific style claim is not sufficient for a broad adaptation question when the relevant profile also contains language, tone, formatting, or answer-detail preferences.
        - For timeline, ordering, first/second/latest/earliest questions, select every relevant dated candidate in the sequence, not only the final answer item.
        - When Planned answer mode is FACTUAL or ACTION_ITEM and Require evidence fallback is false, prefer active typed memory over source candidates.
        - Raw sources are evidence, not current truth. Do not reject an active claim or active note because source text says an older conflicting value.
        - Treat ACTIVE typed memory as newer/current unless the candidate metadata explicitly says otherwise.
        - Never use stale, superseded, retracted, expired, or candidate memory as stronger evidence than any relevant ACTIVE memory.
        - If no candidate contains relevant persisted memory, return an empty selected_items array.

        Planned answer mode: RATIONALE
        Require evidence fallback: false
        Retrieval budget: claims=0 notes=1 actionItems=0 sources=0 episodes=1
        Target message:
        What reusable lesson did we record about ambiguous no-memory recall decisions? Answer with exactly one sentence.

        Candidate memory items:
        1. {"type":"episode","id":"note-consolidation:episode:<0>","score":1.596357142857143,"lifecycle_state":"experience","selection_hint":"Episode memory is reusable experience; prefer for lessons, patterns, and what-worked questions.","text":"situation=Ambiguous recall-intent queries were being handled with a no-memory decision path when ReadTimeRetrievalPlanner returned need_memory=false. action=Use NoMemoryVerifier to run a short model-based verification before accepting the no-memory plan, instead of relying on hard-coded substring heuristics. result=Improved recall-intent detection and reduced brittleness from lexical hacks in Memory MVP retrieval planning. lesson=For ambiguous no-memory recall decisions, prefer a cheap model-based verifier over substring matching.","evidence":[{"source_id":"chat:<1>","kind":"DERIVED_FROM_NOTE","quote":"Lesson: for ambiguous no-memory recall decisions, prefer a cheap model-based verifier over substring matching."}]}
2. {"type":"source","id":"chat:<1>","score":0.6483516483516483,"lifecycle_state":"evidence_for_active_memory","selection_hint":"Evidence only. Select only when exact wording/provenance is required; typed ACTIVE memory carries the current truth.","source_type":"chat_turn","actor_role":"USER","observed_at":"1970-01-01T00:00:00Z","created_at":"1970-01-01T00:00:00Z","retention_class":"STANDARD","usage_policy":"recall=true,evidence=true,extract=true,reason=Contains a reusable engineering lesson and decision rationale for memory-routing behavior.","text":"search_text:\nMemory MVP NoMemoryVerifier lesson: ReadTimeRetrievalPlanner may return need_memory=false for ambiguous recall questions; add a short LLM verification call before accepting no-memory; avoid hard-coded substring heuristics; prefer cheap model-based verifier to catch recall intent.\n\nsource_text:\nMemory MVP lesson for the NoMemoryVerifier component: Situation: ReadTimeRetrievalPlanner can incorrectly return need_memory=false for ambiguous recall questions. Action: run one short LLM verification call before accepting the no-memory plan; do not use hard-coded substring heuristics. Result: we avoid brittle lexical hacks while still catching recall intent. Lesson: for ambiguous no-memory recall decisions, prefer a cheap model-based verifier over substring matching.","supports":[{"type":"claim","id":"note-consolidation:claim:<0>","status":"ACTIVE","lifecycle_state":"current","predicate":"policy_for_ambiguous_no_memory_recall","text":"NoMemoryVerifier policy: for ambiguous recall queries with need_memory=false from ReadTimeRetrievalPlanner, run a brief LLM verification and avoid substring-based heuristics.","evidence_source_ids":["chat:<1>"]},{"type":"note","id":"hot-path:note:<0>","status":"RESOLVED","lifecycle_state":"non_current","title":"NoMemoryVerifier policy for ambiguous no-memory recall decisions","text":"In Memory MVP, if ReadTimeRetrievalPlanner returns need_memory=false on ambiguous recall questions, NoMemoryVerifier should run a short LLM verification before accepting the no-memory plan and avoid hard-coded substring heuristics. This improves recall-intent detection while avoiding brittle lexical...[truncated 7 chars]","evidence_source_ids":["chat:<1>"]},{"type":"episode","id":"note-consolidation:episode:<0>","lifecycle_state":"experience","text":"For ambiguous no-memory recall decisions, prefer a cheap model-based verifier over substring matching.","evidence_source_ids":["chat:<1>"]}]}
3. {"type":"note","id":"hot-path:note:<0>","score":0.46249999999999997,"status":"RESOLVED","lifecycle_state":"non_current","selection_hint":"Non-current note. Use only when the target asks about historical or stale context.","note_type":"LESSON","title":"NoMemoryVerifier policy for ambiguous no-memory recall decisions","text":"In Memory MVP, if ReadTimeRetrievalPlanner returns need_memory=false on ambiguous recall questions, NoMemoryVerifier should run a short LLM verification before accepting the no-memory plan and avoid hard-coded substring heuristics. This improves recall-intent detection while avoiding brittle lexical hacks.","confidence":0.97,"importance":8,"valid_from":"1970-01-01T00:00:00Z","evidence":[{"source_id":"chat:<1>","kind":"DIRECT","quote":"Lesson: for ambiguous no-memory recall decisions, prefer a cheap model-based verifier over substring matching."}]}
4. {"type":"claim","id":"note-consolidation:claim:<0>","score":0.40625,"status":"ACTIVE","lifecycle_state":"current","selection_hint":"Current typed fact. Prefer this for relevant factual answers over raw sources.","predicate":"policy_for_ambiguous_no_memory_recall","subject_entity_id":"entity:<0>","text":"NoMemoryVerifier policy: for ambiguous recall queries with need_memory=false from ReadTimeRetrievalPlanner, run a brief LLM verification and avoid substring-based heuristics.","context":"Memory MVP retrieval-planning behavior","confidence":0.95,"importance":8,"evidence":[{"source_id":"chat:<1>","kind":"DERIVED_FROM_NOTE","quote":"Lesson: for ambiguous no-memory recall decisions, prefer a cheap model-based verifier over substring matching."}]}

        Return JSON:
        {
          "selected_items": [
            {
              "item_type": "claim | note | action_item | profile | source | episode | entity | run",
              "item_id": "exact candidate id",
              "rank": 1,
              "relevance": "direct_answer | supporting_context | required_evidence",
              "reason": "short reason"
            }
          ],
          "rejected_items": [
            {
              "item_type": "claim | note | action_item | profile | source | episode | entity | run",
              "item_id": "exact candidate id",
              "reason": "short reason"
            }
          ],
          "summary": "one short sentence"
        }

responseFormat.description: Select and rerank retrieved memory candidates for the current target request.
responseFormat.name: memory_read_selector

systemPrompts:
You are a concise assistant inside a Gromozeka memory e2e test.
Answer user questions directly.
Do not use tools.
If the question asks for only a short value, return only that value.

Answer user questions directly.
Do not use tools.
If the question asks for only a short value, return only that value.

responseFormat.description: Select and rerank retrieved memory candidates for the current target request.

structured.fullText: {"rejected_items":[{"item_id":"chat:<1>","item_type":"source","reason":"Evidence-only source not needed since typed episode already captures answer and fallback is not required."},{"item_id":"hot-path:note:<0>","item_type":"note","reason":"Non-current resolved note; superseded by concise active experience memory for lesson recall."},{"item_id":"note-consolidation:claim:<0>","item_type":"claim","reason":"Relevant policy fact but less aligned than episode for reusable-lesson wording."}],"selected_items":[{"item_id":"note-consolidation:episode:<0>","item_type":"episode","rank":1,"reason":"Direct reusable lesson phrased for 'what did we record' recall.","relevance":"direct_answer"}],"summary":"Select the episode only because it directly states the recorded reusable lesson and is sufficient for the rationale-style answer."}
